feedback.text: Dear European Commission, As a consumer in the Netherlands, I applaudisseer the Digital Fairness Act as a good step towards consumer protection. However, I have some comments that I would welcome if you consider them. On general consumer protection. At present, the main problem is the fight against symptom, as the problem lies with the owners and shareholders of large companies. As these always strive for higher profits and higher growth, and will never be enough, they will always continue to obtain as much money as possible from consumers, with whatever kind of trucks and designs. The economy is important, but humanity has been neglected by many of them. That said, the following on online markets and digital consumer protection. At present, many types of software always require an internet connection. If this is not inherently necessary for the purpose of the software (such as, for example, purely online gaming), this type of practice should be restricted. Especially in combination with the fact that if a company no longer supports the software, the software does not do it anymore. It is entirely reasonable for a company to decide, after a period of time, not to support their software anymore, but make sure that it remains possible to continue using the software on the latest version. See also Stop Killing Games Companies should not be able to regulate each other on content, as Visa and Mastercard are currently doing with Steam and Itch.io. among others STEAM and Itch.io are pressed by Visa and Mastercard to remove certain products from their catalogue because they are perceived as inappropriate (mainly by problematic conservative Christian groups, in this case Collective Shout from Australia). This opens the door to further pressure from such groups. If someone were to decide what would be inappropriate and not inappropriate, it should be a public body, not a company. I am deeply concerned about the developments in the United Kingdom, with regard to the recently launched online age verification register. On the one hand, many people are trying to circumvent this system, allowing them to walk into the poor of malicious people. On the other hand, a centralised system of personal data is a huge target for hackers and hacker groups, and as the sun system also employs people, mistakes occur, and this can have major consequences. The existence of the sale of Loot Boxes and other systems based on probability. These products operate in the same way as gambling and should therefore be prohibited or regulated in the same way. Monopoly positions of (social) media. In the Netherlands, more and more media sites are owned by the DPG media group. Internationally, for example, Meta is doing the same with social media and communication applications. This gives these companies too much power over the user. Not necessarily to form an opinion, but certainly to determine what is being discussed and what it does not. Consider taking action against monopolies such as these, especially in the media. Thank you for your considerations. We need a strong government in the current international environment, and I hope you can do so.
